description: Pipe materials, schedules, flanges, gaskets, bolts, valves, and welding consumables knowledge for field workers

A106_Gr_B.use: THE most common pipe in refineries and plants — seamless
A106_Gr_B.preheat: None above 32F for thin wall; 200F for wall >1 inch

A53_Gr_B.use: Welded or seamless, utility services, lower-pressure applications, fire water
A53_Gr_B.similar_to: A106 Gr B but less stringent testing
A53_Gr_B.note: ERW (electric resistance welded) seam — oriented seam away from high stress

A333_Gr_6.note: Impact (Charpy) tested at -50F — required for cold service, LPG, propane, refrigeration

API_5L_Gr_B_X42_X52_X60_X65.note: X-grades indicate yield strength in ksi (X52 = 52,000 psi yield)

304_SS_A312_TP304.use: General corrosion resistance, food/pharma, chemical service
304_SS_A312_TP304.sensitization: 800-1500F exposure causes chromium carbide precipitation — use L grades or 321/347 for elevated temperature

304L_SS.use: Same as 304 but low carbon (0.03% max) — resists sensitization better
304L_SS.note: L grade preferred for all welded construction to avoid sensitization at weld HAZ

316_SS_A312_TP316.use: Better corrosion resistance than 304 — chloride environments, more aggressive chemicals, refinery process piping

316L_SS.use: Same as 316 but low carbon — standard for welded refinery stainless piping

321_SS.use: Stabilized with titanium — resists sensitization at elevated temp. Used for high-temperature stainless service.
321_SS.weld_consumables: ER347 (GTAW) — use 347 filler on 321 base (standard practice)

347_SS.use: Stabilized with columbium (niobium) — similar to 321 but better elevated-temp properties

stainless_steel.carbon_contamination_WARNING: NEVER use carbon steel wire brushes, grinding wheels, or tools on stainless steel. Use dedicated stainless tools only. Carbon contamination causes pitting corrosion at contamination sites.
stainless_steel.storage: Store stainless pipe and fittings SEPARATELY from carbon steel. Use wooden cradles or plastic — no direct contact with CS.
stainless_steel.purge_requirement: ALWAYS purge with argon when welding stainless. Sugar (oxidation) on the ID is a REJECTION.

P11_1_25Cr_half_Mo.spec: A335 P11 pipe, A234 WP11 fittings, A182 F11 flanges
P11_1_25Cr_half_Mo.use: High temperature hydrogen service, hydroprocessing units, moderate sulfur environments
P11_1_25Cr_half_Mo.note: All chrome-moly requires PMI verification and strict preheat/PWHT compliance

P22_2_25Cr_1Mo.spec: A335 P22 pipe, A234 WP22 fittings, A182 F22 flanges
P22_2_25Cr_1Mo.use: Higher temperature hydrogen service, catalytic reformer piping, hydrocracker
P22_2_25Cr_1Mo.hardness: Max 225 HBW (Brinell) after PWHT in most refinery specs

P5_5Cr_half_Mo.spec: A335 P5 pipe, A234 WP5 fittings
P5_5Cr_half_Mo.use: High temperature sulfur resistance (naphthenic acid, high temp sulfidation)

P9_9Cr_1Mo.use: Higher sulfur resistance than P5, heater tube piping

P91_9Cr_1Mo_V.spec: A335 P91 pipe, A182 F91 flanges
P91_9Cr_1Mo_V.use: Modern high-temperature power plant and refinery service — replaces P22 in some applications with thinner wall
P91_9Cr_1Mo_V.WARNING: P91 has VERY STRICT welding requirements. Improper welding/PWHT creates Type IV cracking failures.
P91_9Cr_1Mo_V.PWHT: MANDATORY — 1375-1425F, precise temperature control critical. Under-tempering or over-tempering both cause failures.
P91_9Cr_1Mo_V.hardness: 200-275 HBW — too soft or too hard both indicate problems
P91_9Cr_1Mo_V.weld_consumables: Matching P91 filler metal ONLY — do NOT substitute
special_requirements: Qualified P91 welders only (not all chrome-moly welders can weld P91)
Thermocouple monitoring of preheat, interpass, AND PWHT
Hardness testing of ALL welds after PWHT
No quenching — must slow cool to below 200F before PWHT
PWHT must be between 1375-1425F — out of range = potential failure in service

2205_UNS_S31803_S32205.use: Chloride environments where austenitic SS would crack. Seawater, offshore, brine, brackish water.
2205_UNS_S31803_S32205.weld_consumables: ER2209 (GTAW) — over-alloyed filler to maintain phase balance
2205_UNS_S31803_S32205.preheat: None required (do NOT preheat above 300F — damages phase balance)
2205_UNS_S31803_S32205.PWHT: Generally NOT required and can be HARMFUL
2205_UNS_S31803_S32205.max_interpass: 300F maximum — CRITICAL. Excessive heat damages phase balance (50/50 ferrite/austenite).
2205_UNS_S31803_S32205.ferrite_testing: Ferrite content 35-65% required in weld and HAZ. Tested with ferrite scope.
special_notes: Heat input CRITICAL — too high = sigma phase (brittle). Too low = excessive ferrite.
Typical heat input range: 0.5 - 2.5 kJ/mm depending on thickness
Do NOT use pure CO2 shield gas — use Ar or Ar/N2 mix
N2 addition to purge gas (90% Ar, 10% N2) helps maintain austenite in root

super_duplex_2507.use: More aggressive chloride environments than 2205 — high-pressure seawater, aggressive chemical service
super_duplex_2507.notes: Even more critical welding controls than 2205. Specialist welders required.

alloy_20_UNS_N08020.use: Sulfuric acid service — THE acid alloy. Alky units, sulfuric acid piping.
alloy_20_UNS_N08020.weld_consumables: ERNiCrMo-3 (Alloy 625 filler) or matching Alloy 20 filler
alloy_20_UNS_N08020.notes: Nickel alloy — expensive. Handle with care, no carbon steel contamination. Purge with argon.

Inconel_625_UNS_N06625.use: Extreme corrosion resistance — acid gas, high temp oxidation, chloride, seawater
Inconel_625_UNS_N06625.notes: VERY expensive. Used in worst-case corrosive services.

Inconel_600_UNS_N06600.use: High temperature oxidation and carburization resistance

pipe_schedules_and_wall_thickness.description: Wall thickness increases with schedule number. Same schedule number = thicker wall on larger pipe.

common_schedules.SCH_5S: Thin wall stainless — low pressure utility services
common_schedules.SCH_10S: Light stainless applications — typical for 150# stainless utility
common_schedules.SCH_40_STD: STANDARD weight — the default for most carbon steel applications
common_schedules.SCH_80_XS: Extra strong — higher pressure or where corrosion allowance needed
common_schedules.SCH_160: Very heavy wall — high pressure service

reference_wall_thickness_inches.note: Selected sizes showing wall thickness progression
reference_wall_thickness_inches.important_note: Above 12 inch, STD and SCH 40 are NOT the same. STD stays at 0.375 inch wall for all sizes above 12 inch. SCH 40 continues to increase. Always specify schedule number OR wall thickness, never assume.

flange_ratings.pressure_class_explanation: Flange ratings (Class/Pound) define the maximum pressure at a given temperature. Higher temperature = lower pressure rating.

150_lb.common_use: Low pressure utility — water, air, low-pressure steam, cooling water

300_lb.common_use: Medium pressure process piping, steam systems

600_lb.common_use: High pressure process — hydroprocessing, high-pressure steam

900_lb.face_type: Often RTJ (Ring Type Joint) at 900# and above

1500_lb.common_use: Very high pressure — wellhead piping, high-pressure let-down

2500_lb.common_use: Extreme pressure — urea synthesis, ammonia synthesis, some wellhead

flange_face_types.RF_raised_face: Most common. Gasket seals on raised portion. Standard for 150# and 300#.
flange_face_types.RTJ_ring_type_joint: Metal ring groove machined into face. Used for high-pressure (typically 600# and above mandatory for 900#+). Most reliable seal.
flange_face_types.FF_flat_face: Full face contact. Used for cast iron flanges, non-metallic flanges. NEVER bolt flat face to raised face without machining — will crack the flat face flange.
flange_face_types.tongue_and_groove: Interlocking faces. Less common, used in specialty applications.
flange_face_types.CRITICAL_NOTE: NEVER mix flange face types without engineering approval. RF to FF bolting without machining = cracked flange or leak.

B7_A193_B7.use: THE standard stud bolt for high-temperature, high-pressure service in refineries

B7M_A193_B7M.material: Same as B7 but lower hardness (235 HBW max vs 321 HBW for B7)
B7M_A193_B7M.use: Sour service (wet H2S) where NACE MR0175/ISO 15156 applies — prevents sulfide stress cracking
B7M_A193_B7M.note: REQUIRED in sour (H2S) service — standard B7 too hard for sour

L7_A320_L7.material: Similar to B7 but impact tested for low-temperature service

classes.Class_1: Solution annealed — LOW strength (75,000 psi tensile). Used where stainless required for corrosion.
classes.Class_2: Strain hardened — HIGHER strength (100,000-125,000 psi). Closer to B7 strength.

B8_A193_B8.use: Where corrosion resistance requires stainless bolts
B8_A193_B8.CAUTION: Class 1 is MUCH weaker than B7. Cannot use B7 torque values. Requires lower torque.

B8M_A193_B8M.use: Better corrosion resistance than B8 — chloride, marine environments

bolt_specifications.anti_galling: ALL stainless bolts (B8, B8M) MUST use anti-seize compound. Stainless on stainless galls and seizes without lubricant. Do NOT use copper-based anti-seize on stainless in high-temp service (copper contamination causes embrittlement). Use nickel-based or molybdenum-based anti-seize.

gate_valve.function: On-off isolation (full open or full closed — NOT for throttling)
gate_valve.design: Rising stem, wedge or double-disc gate
gate_valve.advantages: Full bore — low pressure drop when fully open, piggable
gate_valve.disadvantages: Cannot throttle (erosion, vibration), slow operation, large space for stem travel
gate_valve.common_specs: API 600 (cast steel), API 602 (forged steel, small bore)
gate_valve.installation_note: Install with stem vertical or above horizontal. Never inverted (debris settles in bonnet).

globe_valve.function: Throttling and flow regulation, also used for isolation
globe_valve.design: Rising stem, disc seats against body seat
globe_valve.advantages: Excellent throttling, positive shutoff, can be repaired in-line
globe_valve.disadvantages: High pressure drop (flow makes two 90-degree turns), not full bore
globe_valve.installation_note: Flow direction matters — typically pressure under disc (check arrow on body). Install stem UP.

ball_valve.function: Quick on-off isolation, some designs suitable for throttling

types.full_port: Bore same as pipe ID — low pressure drop, piggable
types.reduced_port: Bore one size smaller — acceptable for many applications, lighter/cheaper
types.V_port: Characterized ball for throttling — better than standard ball for flow control

ball_valve.advantages: Fast operation (quarter turn), tight shutoff, low pressure drop (full port), compact
ball_valve.disadvantages: Not great for throttling (standard), seats damaged by debris, expensive in large sizes/high ratings
ball_valve.installation_note: Handle position indicates open/closed — verify. Support weight on both sides.

types.swing_check: Hinged disc — low pressure drop, common in horizontal lines. Can be vertical FLOW-UP only.
types.piston_check: Piston/dash-pot — can be installed horizontally or vertically flow-up. Better for pulsating flow.
types.dual_plate_wafer: Two spring-loaded plates — compact, lighter, installed between flanges. Good for lines with frequent reversal.
types.tilting_disc: Pivoted disc — fast closing, low pressure drop. Used where water hammer from slow-closing check is concern.

check_valve.installation_note: ALWAYS verify flow direction arrow. Wrong orientation = valve doesn't function. Swing checks MUST be horizontal or vertical flow-up (never vertical flow-down).

butterfly_valve.function: On-off and throttling, large line sizes
butterfly_valve.advantages: Light weight, compact, low cost in large sizes, quick operation
butterfly_valve.disadvantages: Not full bore (disc always in flow path), limited shutoff class, not for high pressure

types.concentric: Standard, lower pressure rating, rubber seat
types.triple_offset: Premium — metal-to-metal seat, bubble-tight shutoff, rated for high pressure/temperature

butterfly_valve.installation_note: Check disc clearance in pipe — gasket ID must not protrude into bore. Wafer style needs alignment pins.

plug_valve.design: Tapered or cylindrical plug with port

types.lubricated: Sealant injected to maintain seal — common in pipeline service
types.non_lubricated: Sleeve-lined — used in chemical service where lubricant contamination unacceptable

plug_valve.advantages: Fast quarter-turn operation, multi-port options, good for slurry/dirty service
plug_valve.installation_note: Verify port orientation — can be three-way or four-way in some designs

E6010.use: Root pass on carbon steel pipe — the pipe fitter's rod
E6010.key_properties: Deep penetration, fast freeze, forceful arc — excellent for open root pipe welding
E6010.storage: No oven needed — cellulosic coating needs moisture to function properly
E6010.note: Cannot be used on chrome-moly or where low hydrogen required

E7018.use: Fill and cap passes on carbon steel, also root pass on some procedures. THE general-purpose structural rod.
E7018.key_properties: Low hydrogen — prevents hydrogen cracking. Smooth arc, good appearance.
E7018.storage: MUST be stored in holding oven at 250-300F after opening sealed container. Max 4 hours out of oven (some specs 2 hours). If exposed longer, re-bake per manufacturer instructions.
E7018.CRITICAL: Moisture in low-hydrogen rods causes hydrogen cracking — ALWAYS keep dry. This is the #1 cause of cracking in field welding.

E8018_B2.type: Low hydrogen for 1-1/4 Cr-1/2 Mo (P4 material)
E8018_B2.use: Fill and cap on P11 chrome-moly pipe
E8018_B2.storage: Same low-hydrogen storage as 7018 — oven at 250-300F

E9018_B3.type: Low hydrogen for 2-1/4 Cr-1 Mo (P5A material)
E9018_B3.use: Fill and cap on P22 chrome-moly pipe

E309L_16.use: Welding carbon steel to stainless steel (transition welds), first layer of stainless overlay on carbon steel
E309L_16.note: Over-alloyed to account for dilution from carbon steel

ER70S_2.use: Carbon steel GTAW — most common for pipe root passes
ER70S_2.note: Triple deoxidized — excellent for root passes with variable fit-up

ER70S_6.use: Carbon steel GTAW and GMAW — higher silicon for better wetting
ER70S_6.note: Better for GMAW than 70S-2; 70S-2 preferred for GTAW pipe roots

ER308L.note: L grade (low carbon) standard to prevent sensitization

ER309L.use: GTAW filler for dissimilar metal welds (CS to SS), first layer of stainless overlay
ER309L.note: Higher chromium and nickel to compensate for dilution with carbon steel
ER309L.IMPORTANT: Use 309L for the FIRST pass when joining CS to SS. Switch to 308L or 316L for subsequent passes.

ER316L.note: Molybdenum content provides better pitting/crevice corrosion resistance than 308L

ER2209.use: GTAW filler for duplex stainless steel 2205
ER2209.note: Over-alloyed (22% Cr, 9% Ni) to maintain proper ferrite/austenite phase balance in weld

ERNiCrMo_3.use: GTAW filler for Inconel, Alloy 20, nickel alloy overlays, dissimilar metals involving nickel alloys
ERNiCrMo_3.note: Premium filler — expensive but very versatile for exotic alloys

consumable_storage_summary.low_hydrogen_SMAW: 250-300F holding oven. Max exposure time 4 hours (many sites say 2 hours). Re-bake if exceeded.
consumable_storage_summary.cellulosic_SMAW_6010: Room temperature storage. Sealed container until use. Do NOT put in oven.
consumable_storage_summary.GTAW_wire: Clean, dry storage. Keep in tube/container. Wipe with acetone before use. Handle with clean gloves — oil from hands = porosity.
consumable_storage_summary.FCAW_wire: Sealed container until use. Some wires require heated storage (check manufacturer). Rusty wire = porosity.
